intro: Second-person framing. This one is real Retrofit and real OkHttp talking to a real local server (MockWebServer), not a stand-in. The User/NewUserRequest models and the UserApi/LegacyApi/CreateUserApi interfaces in Tasks.kt are given, genuine @GET/@Path/@Query/@POST/@Body annotations - worth reading closely since that annotation surface IS what this topic tests. Their job is a handful of functions marked TODO that build the Retrofit client or handle its edge cases. Each task has a failing test in src/test to turn green, run one at a time from the gutter in Android Studio or with ./gradlew :retrofit-api:test --tests "*ClassName".

tasks.title: createUserApi - build a real Retrofit client
tasks.detail: UserApi.getUser is a real suspend fun with @GET, @Path and @Query. Build a Retrofit instance pointed at baseUrl with a kotlinx.serialization JSON converter attached, then create() a UserApi from it.
tasks.verify: T1CreateUserApiTest is green: a user fetched from MockWebServer decodes correctly, and the query param shows up in the request path MockWebServer actually received.

tasks.title: createLegacyApi - the leading-slash baseUrl gotcha
tasks.detail: Retrofit.Builder.baseUrl throws IllegalArgumentException if the string doesn't end in a slash. Normalize baseUrl (append one if it's missing) before building the client for LegacyApi the same way as createUserApi.
tasks.verify: T2LeadingSlashTest is green: a baseUrl missing its trailing slash still builds and resolves correctly, and (separately, no extra code needed) LegacyApi's leading-slash @GET path is shown dropping baseUrl's own path segment.

tasks.title: createCreateUserApi - the same client shape, for a POST
tasks.detail: Same Retrofit-building code as createUserApi, this time create()-ing CreateUserApi, whose createUser suspend fun POSTs a @Serializable NewUserRequest as the @Body.
tasks.hint: Same Retrofit.Builder shape as t1, just create(CreateUserApi::class.java) instead.
tasks.verify: T3CreateUserBodyTest is green: the JSON body MockWebServer actually received decodes back to the exact NewUserRequest that was sent, and the Content-Type header says application/json.

tasks.title: fetchUserResult - map a 404 to a typed result
tasks.detail: api.getUser(id) (the bare-User suspend fun) throws HttpException on any non-2xx response. Catch it: e.code() == 404 becomes NotFound, anything else becomes Failed(e.code()). A successful call becomes Found.
tasks.hint: try { UserResult.Found(api.getUser(id)) } catch (e: HttpException) { if (e.code() == 404) UserResult.NotFound(id) else UserResult.Failed(e.code()) }
tasks.verify: T4TypedErrorTest is green: a 404 maps to NotFound, a 500 maps to Failed(500), a success maps to Found, and (no extra code needed) api.getUserResponse is shown never throwing at all for that same 404, just coming back with isSuccessful == false.

tasks.title: authHeaderInterceptor - a real OkHttp Interceptor
tasks.detail: Return an Interceptor that builds a new request with an Authorization: Bearer <token> header (calling tokenProvider() fresh each time, not once at construction) and continues the chain.
tasks.verify: T5AuthInterceptorTest is green: the Authorization header MockWebServer actually received matches the token, and it changes between two calls when tokenProvider's return value changes.

tasks.title: refreshingAuthenticator - refresh on 401, retry once
tasks.detail: Return an Authenticator: on a 401, call refreshToken() and rebuild the request with the refreshed header. priorResponseCount (given) tells you how many times this chain has already retried - once it's 2 or more, return null so OkHttp gives up instead of looping forever.
tasks.verify: T6RefreshAuthenticatorTest is green: a 401 followed by a 200 succeeds with the refreshed token on the retry, and two 401s in a row stop after exactly one retry instead of looping.
